title: Getting Started with the Hiverse
text:
1 Why this book?
The Hudson “hiverse” is powerful, but it is not flat. A few packages are useful almost every week, a few are important for platform work, and a few are specialist tools that only matter in the right problem domain.
This book is designed as a self-study guide for new Isazi employees. It focuses on:
You do not need to learn every package at once. The recommended path is:

title: Getting Started with the Hiverse
text:
1.1 The package map





flowchart LR
  A["Raw files, tables, process outputs"] --> B["himunge"]
  A --> C["hiconnect"]
  B --> D["higrate"]
  C --> D
  B --> E["himodel"]
  C --> F["hiproc"]
  B --> G["hisched"]
  H["LLM providers"] --> I["higpt"]
  I --> D
  I --> J["hidrogen"]
  L["Time-based analysis"] --> M["hidate"]
  M --> E
  M --> G
  N["Signals and monitoring"] --> O["hipoint"]

title: Getting Started with the Hiverse
text:
1.2 How to use this book

Read the overviews quickly.
Type the examples yourself if you can.
Do the exercises with your own variations.
Keep notes on what felt easy, awkward, or surprisingly useful.

Each chapter has a few activities:

Try it: a direct exercise to build fluency.
Stretch: a more open-ended task closer to real project work.
Think: a short reflection prompt about tradeoffs or workflow design.

title: Getting Started with the Hiverse
text:
1.3 What this book assumes

You can run R locally.
You have access to GitLab and a personal access token.
You may or may not have live Hudson database access yet.

Where an exercise needs a live database or an API key, it is marked clearly. Whenever possible, the examples use small local data frames or package sample data instead.

title: Getting Started with the Hiverse
text:
1.4 Packages covered
Deep-dive chapters:

hiconnect
himunge
hidate
hilog
hiproc
higpt
higrate
hidrogen
himodel
hipoint
hisched

Supporting package catalog:

hiplot
hilog
hifile
hidoc
hidash
himatch
higest
hidalgo

title: Getting Started with the Hiverse
text:
1.5 Suggested study tracks








Track
Packages
Why start here?




Core data scientist track
himunge, hiconnect, hidate, hilog, hiproc, himodel
These packages are closest to day-to-day Hudson analysis and platform work.


AI-era extension track
higpt, higrate, hidrogen
These packages add leverage once you already understand the underlying data and workflows.


Specialist track
hipoint, hisched
High-value in the right domain, but not required for every new joiner immediately.


Supporting tools track
hiplot, hifile, hidoc, hidash, himatch, higest, hidalgo
Useful context and workflow support, but not the best first investment for most people.

title: Getting Started with the Hiverse
text:
1.6 What success looks like
By the end of the book, a new team member should be able to:

install the hiverse packages cleanly
inspect a new dataset and reason about keys, quality, and relationships
understand the difference between data work, platform work, and AI workflow tooling
decide which package to reach for before starting a task
complete one small end-to-end exploration using the Hudson stack

title: 2  Setup and Installation
text:
3 Install the packages the Hudson way
Most Hudson packages live in GitLab, so the first hurdle is not R code. It is package access and installation discipline.

title: 2  Setup and Installation
section: 3.1 Step 1: create a GitLab personal access token
text:
3.1 Step 1: create a GitLab personal access token
At a high level:

Open your GitLab account settings.
Go to the access token area.
Create a token with the scopes needed to read packages and repositories.
Store it safely.

Then add it to ~/.Renviron:

usethis::edit_r_environ()

Add a line like:
GITLAB_PAT=glpat-your-token-goes-here
Restart R after saving.






Warning



Do not hard-code tokens in scripts, notebooks, or project files.

title: 2  Setup and Installation
text:
3.2 Step 2: install hiverse
hiverse is the meta-package that gives you a convenient entry point.

install.packages("devtools")
devtools::install_gitlab("isazi/hudson-packages/hiverse@main")
library(hiverse)

title: 2  Setup and Installation
section: 3.3 Step 3: install the common package set
text:
3.3 Step 3: install the common package set
hiverse can install the common packages for you:

hiverse::install_hudson_packages("common")

That common set currently includes the main packages new employees are most likely to use first, such as hiconnect, himunge, hiproc, hidate, hilog, higpt, higrate, hidrogen, and himodel.

title: 2  Setup and Installation
section: 3.4 Step 4: install optional packages only when needed
text:
3.4 Step 4: install optional packages only when needed
Some packages are more specialist and should usually be installed when the work demands them:

devtools::install_gitlab("isazi/hudson-packages/hipoint")
devtools::install_gitlab("isazi/hudson-packages/hisched")

Other optional packages exist as well, especially for supply-chain work, but you do not need all of them on day one.

title: 2  Setup and Installation
2  Setup and Installation
text:
3.6 Recommended learning order
If you are a new joiner and want the shortest path to competence:

himunge
hiconnect
hidate
hilog
hiproc if you touch processes
higpt
higrate
hidrogen
himodel
hipoint or hisched if your project calls for them

title: 2  Setup and Installation
text:
3.7 Try it

Install hiverse.
Install the common packages.
Verify that requireNamespace("himunge", quietly = TRUE) returns TRUE.
Write down which packages failed to install, if any, and why.

title: 2  Setup and Installation
text:
3.8 Stretch
Create a small script called check_hiverse_install.R that:

checks a package list
prints which packages are installed
prints sessionInfo() at the end

This is a good first “works on my machine” diagnostic to keep around.

title: 2  Setup and Installation
text:
3.9 Think
Why is hiverse useful even if you could install every package directly with devtools::install_gitlab()?

title: 3  Core Data Packages
text:
4 The packages you will use most often
For most data scientists at Isazi, the center of gravity is:
If you become fluent with these four, the rest of the hiverse becomes easier.

title: 3  Core Data Packages
section: 4.1 himunge: your first stop for unfamiliar data
text:
4.1 himunge: your first stop for unfamiliar data
himunge is the strongest all-round package in the ecosystem. It is useful because it helps with the actual first questions people ask about a dataset:

what do these columns mean?
what looks like a key?
which columns determine which others?
where is the data inconsistent?


4.1.1 A tiny messy dataset
The following chunk creates a toy order table with a hidden key problem:

orders_dirty <- data.frame(
  record_id = 1:6,
  customer_id = c("C001", "C001", "C003", "C004", "C002", "C002"),
  order_id = c("O100", "O101", "O300", "O400", "O200", "O200"),
  order_date = c("2025-01-03", "2025-01-08", "2025-02-01", "2025-02-05", "2025-01-10", "2025-01-10"),
  order_status = c("shipped", "processing", "shipped", "processing", "processing", "cancelled"),
  amount = c("1,200", "950", "2,000", "500", "850", "850"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

dup_counts <- aggregate(
  rep(1, nrow(orders_dirty)),
  by = list(order_id = orders_dirty$order_id),
  FUN = length
)

barplot(
  dup_counts$x,
  names.arg = dup_counts$order_id,
  col = "#0b6e69",
  main = "Rows per order_id in the toy dataset",
  ylab = "Count"
)
abline(h = 1, lty = 2, col = "#a44a1f")








The point is not the bar chart itself. The point is that duplicate-looking keys are one of the fastest ways to discover real data problems.


4.1.2 Example workflow

library(himunge)

clean_orders <- orders_dirty |>
  himunge::fix_column_names() |>
  himunge::convert_hidden_dates() |>
  himunge::convert_hidden_numerics()

invisible(utils::capture.output({
  key_candidates <- himunge::find_primary_keys(clean_orders)
}))

viol_cols <- himunge::find_cols_violating_key(
  clean_orders,
  cols = c("order_id")
)

viol <- suppressWarnings(
  himunge::find_indices_violating_key(
    clean_orders,
    candidate_key = c("order_id"),
    make_reduced_dataframe = TRUE
  )
)

key_candidates

[[1]]
[1] "record_id"

[[2]]
[1] "order_id"     "order_status"

[[3]]
[1] "order_date"   "order_status"

[[4]]
[1] "amount"       "order_status"

viol_cols

                      col severity
record_id       record_id        2
order_status order_status        2

viol$data

  order_id record_id order_status
6     O200         6    cancelled




4.1.3 Why this matters
This is the kind of workflow that helps a data scientist answer:

“Is this really one row per order?”
“Which columns disagree when the key stays fixed?”
“Do I trust this table enough to model from it?”



4.1.4 Try it

Add a sales_rep column that is perfectly determined by customer_id.
Run himunge::get_column_dependency_tree() on the cleaned data.
Explain which columns appear downstream and why.



4.1.5 Stretch
Build a new toy admissions dataset with columns like:

student_id
campus
programme
intake_month
fee_band
advisor

Then:

use find_primary_keys()
introduce a deliberate key violation
use find_indices_violating_key() to isolate the bad rows
write a one-paragraph summary of the issue as if you were reporting it to a client

title: 3  Core Data Packages
section: 4.2 hiconnect: the main route into Hudson data
text:
4.2 hiconnect: the main route into Hudson data
hiconnect matters because most real Hudson work eventually touches a database. It is not just a thin wrapper over DBI; it encodes Hudson conventions around schemas, outputs, and process configuration.

4.2.1 The important idea
Before you can query anything, you usually need a valid process config file. The package README calls this out explicitly.

library(hiconnect)

generate_first_hiconnect_config(
  host = "your-host",
  user = "your-user",
  password = "your-password"
)

Then the common workflow is:

con <- hiconnect::pg_connect(c("scratch", "process"))
on.exit(DBI::dbDisconnect(con), add = TRUE)

row_count <- hiconnect::pg_count("scratch", "some_table", con = con)
sample_tbl <- hiconnect::pg_get_table("scratch", "some_table", limit = 100, con = con)



4.2.2 How to think about hiconnect
Use it when you need to:

inspect tables quickly
read or write Hudson data safely
build process-aware code
avoid hand-written connection boilerplate in every script



4.2.3 Try it
This exercise needs Hudson database access.

Connect to one schema you are allowed to access.
Pick one table and count its rows.
Pull the first few rows.
Identify what you believe the primary key might be before using himunge.



4.2.4 Stretch
Use hiconnect and himunge together:

read a table from Hudson
propose a key with find_primary_keys()
check whether any columns violate that key
write down whether the table looks model-ready

title: 3  Core Data Packages
text:
4.3 hilog: log early, log clearly
hilog is small, but it is worth learning early because it encourages a good habit: code should say what it is doing while it runs.
That matters in:

long-running scripts
data-quality audits
package code
Hudson processes
debugging interactive work that later becomes production code


4.3.1 The core idea
The logging functions support sprintf-style interpolation directly through their ... arguments, so you can write concise, structured messages without building strings manually.

suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(hilog))

hilog::set_level_info()

hilog::info("Loaded %i rows from %s", 1250, "orders_extract.csv")

[2026-03-17 00:03:21 eval/eval] INFO: Loaded 1250 rows from orders_extract.csv

hilog::info("Coverage after cleaning: %.1f%%", 97.4)

[2026-03-17 00:03:21 eval/eval] INFO: Coverage after cleaning: 97.4%


That style is worth copying. It keeps the message template stable and pushes the changing values into arguments.
A typical emitted line looks like:
[2026-03-16 10:15:00 audit_orders] INFO: Starting order audit on 6 rows and 5 columns
The timestamp and calling context come for free, which is why this is more useful than scattering print() calls through your code.


4.3.2 A more realistic example

audit_orders <- function(df) {
  hilog::info("Starting order audit on %i rows and %i columns", nrow(df), ncol(df))

  invisible(utils::capture.output({
    key_sets <- himunge::find_primary_keys(df)
  }))
  hilog::info("Found %i candidate key sets", length(key_sets))

  if (length(key_sets) > 0) {
    hilog::info("Smallest candidate key: %s", paste(key_sets[[1]], collapse = ", "))
  } else {
    hilog::error("No primary key candidate found for current dataset")
  }
}

audit_orders(clean_orders)

[2026-03-17 00:03:21 eval/audit_orders] INFO: Starting order audit on 6 rows and 6 columns
[2026-03-17 00:03:21 eval/audit_orders] INFO: Found 4 candidate key sets
[2026-03-17 00:03:21 eval/audit_orders] INFO: Smallest candidate key: record_id


In practice, that means you do not need to guess where a script got to before it failed or started behaving strangely.


4.3.3 Debug level

hilog::set_level_debug()
hilog::debug("Duplicate order_id count: %i", sum(duplicated(orders_dirty$order_id)))

[2026-03-17 00:03:21 eval/eval] DEBUG: Duplicate order_id count: 1


Use info() for progress, debug() for extra detail, and error() for messages that should always be visible.


4.3.4 Try it

Add hilog::info() calls to one of your own analysis scripts.
Log:

how many rows were loaded
how many columns were selected
what key you think the table has

Rewrite one message using %i, %s, or %.2f formatting instead of paste().



4.3.5 Stretch
Take the earlier himunge toy exercise and add logging at each step:

raw row count
cleaned row count
number of candidate key sets found
whether violations were detected

Then imagine that script runs overnight. Which log lines would help you most the next morning?

title: 3  Core Data Packages
section: 4.4 hidate: small package, very high practical value
text:
4.4 hidate: small package, very high practical value
Many Isazi projects are date-heavy. Forecast cutoffs, planning windows, working days, weekly rollups, and month boundaries show up constantly.

4.4.1 Simple example

library(hidate)

date <- as.Date("2026-03-16")

hidate::start_of_month(date)

[1] "2026-03-01"

hidate::end_of_month(date)

[1] "2026-03-31"

hidate::start_of_week(date)

[1] "2026-03-16"

hidate::end_of_week(date)

[1] "2026-03-22"

hidate::diff_weekdays(c(as.Date("2026-03-16"), as.Date("2026-03-31")))

[1] 11




4.4.2 A visual: working-day accumulation

march_days <- seq(as.Date("2026-03-01"), as.Date("2026-03-31"), by = "day")
weekday_flag <- weekdays(march_days) %in% c("Monday", "Tuesday", "Wednesday", "Thursday", "Friday")
cum_weekdays <- cumsum(weekday_flag)

plot(
  march_days,
  cum_weekdays,
  type = "l",
  lwd = 3,
  col = "#0b6e69",
  xlab = "Date",
  ylab = "Cumulative weekdays",
  main = "Why date helpers matter in planning work"
)
grid()










4.4.3 Try it

Pick a date relevant to your project.
Compute its month start, month end, week start, and week end.
Work out how many weekdays remain in that month.



4.4.4 Stretch
Write a function called planning_window() that returns:

the current week start
the current month end
the number of weekdays between them

Then compare your result with hidate::diff_weekdays().

title: 3  Core Data Packages
text:
4.5 Think
Which is riskier on a real project:

getting the model wrong
or getting the date logic wrong before the model is even run?

title: 4  Platform Workflows
text:
5 Packages for Hudson process work
Not every analyst needs these every day. But if you work close to the Hudson platform, they matter a lot.

title: 4  Platform Workflows
section: 5.1 hiproc: understanding processes, queues, and dependencies
text:
5.1 hiproc: understanding processes, queues, and dependencies
hiproc is important because Hudson is not just a collection of tables. It is also a process system. When something fails, runs late, or produces a strange output, you often need process context, not just data context.

5.1.1 What hiproc is good for

summarising process events
looking up dependencies
inspecting files and run parameters
understanding what happened before and after a process run



5.1.2 Example
The book renders this example against a tiny mocked process registry so that the actual hiproc calls can run without Hudson access.

library(hiproc)

offline_process_view <- with_mocked_hiconnect_pg_get_query(mock_process_pg_get_query, {
  list(
    events = hiproc::event_summary(),
    deps = hiproc::get_dependencies(
      "build_dashboard",
      recursive = TRUE,
      con = structure(list(), class = "mock_con")
    )
  )
})

offline_process_view$events[, c("process_name", "last_status", "diagnostic")]

# A tibble: 2 × 3
  process_name    last_status diagnostic                   
  <chr>           <chr>       <chr>                        
1 build_dashboard Succeeded   Latest run was 2-3 months ago
2 ingest_orders   Failed      Latest run Failed!           

offline_process_view$deps$process[, c("name", "dependency_process_name", "depth")]

             name dependency_process_name depth
1 build_dashboard           ingest_orders     1




5.1.3 Process thinking





flowchart TD
  A["Raw input file arrives"] --> B["Ingestion process runs"]
  B --> C["Table is updated"]
  C --> D["Downstream process runs"]
  D --> E["Dashboard or forecast changes"]
  F["hiproc"] --> B
  F --> D






When work breaks in production, this is the package that helps you move from “the number looks wrong” to “which process or dependency likely caused it?”


5.1.4 Try it
This exercise needs platform access.

Run event_summary().
Pick one process that looks active or familiar.
Inspect its dependencies.
Sketch the upstream/downstream flow in your own words.



5.1.5 Stretch
Choose one process output table and one upstream dependency. Ask:

what data enters here?
what process transforms it?
what would fail silently vs loudly?

Then write a short incident note as if you were on support duty.

title: 4  Platform Workflows
text:
5.2 higest: standardised ingestion logic
higest is not a day-one analyst package, but it is useful once you start building repeatable ingestion flows.
The package frames ingestion as four steps:

prepare
read
standardise
write


5.2.1 Example pipeline shape
The full prepare() path also needs a process config file and a writable Hudson target. For a day-one local sandbox, you can start one step later with a tiny spec and still run the package pipeline end to end.

library(higest)

tmp_csv <- tempfile(fileext = ".csv")
write.csv(
  data.frame(order_id = c("O100", "O101"), amount = c(1200, 950)),
  tmp_csv,
  row.names = FALSE
)

my_format_df <- data.frame(
  orig_name = c("order_id", "amount"),
  clean_name = c("order_id", "amount"),
  final_name = c("order_id", "amount"),
  format = c("character", "numeric"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

pipeline <- list(
  ingestion_process_name = "toy_orders",
  file = list(path = tmp_csv),
  format_df = my_format_df,
  output_table = list(schema = "scratch", table = "toy_orders")
)
class(pipeline) <- "higest_spec"

pipeline <- pipeline |>
  higest::add_read_spec_csv() |>
  higest::add_transformation_identity()

pipeline$write_spec <- list(type = "collect")
pipeline$write_fn <- function(hs) hs$final_data

invisible(utils::capture.output({
  result <- higest::run_higest_pipeline(pipeline)
}))

result$result

  order_id amount
1     O100   1200
2     O101    950


The important lesson is not the exact function names. It is the idea that ingestion should be declared and inspectable, not hidden inside a one-off script.

title: 4  Platform Workflows
text:
5.3 Other platform-adjacent helpers

hifile: import/export helpers for files and file tables
hidoc: report and data-dictionary helpers
hidash: dashboard helpers for Shiny-based Hudson work
hilog: simple logging utilities that support package and process code

You do not need to master these immediately, but you should know they exist.

title: 4  Platform Workflows
text:
5.4 Think
When a stakeholder says “the dashboard changed unexpectedly”, what is the right first question:

“Which model did we use?”
or “Which process ran, with which inputs, and what changed upstream?”

title: 5  AI-Era Extensions
text:
6 The newer AI layer
These packages sit on top of the more traditional Hudson stack.

title: 5  AI-Era Extensions
text:
6.1 higpt: the foundation layer
If you only learn one AI package well, learn higpt.
It is the main LLM interface package for the ecosystem and supports:

chat
tool calling
structured outputs
embeddings
image and audio workflows
multiple providers


6.1.1 Two useful mental models

continue_chat() and chat_with_tools() are the lower-level interfaces.
make_model() |> ... |> submit() is the newer tidy DSL.
The builder DSL is usually the better teaching surface because it keeps prompts, schemas, tools, logging, and async settings in one object.

The live versions need API keys. The runnable book examples below swap in mock provider responses so you can still see the workflow offline.

library(higpt)

capable_models <- subset(
  higpt::get_available_models("continue_chat"),
  structured_outputs | supports_vision,
  select = c(model, api, structured_outputs, supports_vision)
)

utils::head(capable_models, 6)

# A tibble: 6 × 4
  model                  api    structured_outputs supports_vision
  <chr>                  <chr>  <lgl>              <lgl>          
1 gpt-5.4                OpenAI TRUE               TRUE           
2 gpt-5.4-2026-03-05     OpenAI TRUE               TRUE           
3 gpt-5.4-pro            OpenAI FALSE              TRUE           
4 gpt-5.4-pro-2026-03-05 OpenAI FALSE              TRUE           
5 gpt-5.3-chat-latest    OpenAI TRUE               TRUE           
6 gpt-5.3-codex          OpenAI TRUE               TRUE           




6.1.2 Model aliases: -latest and friends
One of the most practical higpt features is that many model names are aliases rather than hard-coded vendor IDs.

alias_models <- subset(
  higpt::get_available_models("continue_chat"),
  model %in% c(
    "gpt-latest",
    "gpt-chat-latest",
    "gpt-mini-latest",
    "claude-sonnet-latest",
    "gemini-pro-latest",
    "grok-latest"
  ),
  select = c(model, api, api_model, category)
)

alias_models

# A tibble: 6 × 4
  model                api       api_model              category      
  <chr>                <chr>     <chr>                  <chr>         
1 claude-sonnet-latest Anthropic claude-sonnet-4-6      ChatCompletion
2 gpt-latest           OpenAI    gpt-5.4                Reasoning     
3 gpt-mini-latest      OpenAI    gpt-5-mini             Reasoning     
4 gpt-chat-latest      OpenAI    gpt-5.3-chat-latest    Reasoning     
5 gemini-pro-latest    Gemini    gemini-3.1-pro-preview ChatCompletion
6 grok-latest          Grok      grok-4.20-beta         Reasoning     


As of 2026-03-17 in the current package registry, those aliases resolve to concrete IDs via the api_model column. For example:

gpt-latest currently resolves to gpt-5.4
gpt-chat-latest currently resolves to gpt-5.3-chat-latest
claude-sonnet-latest currently resolves to claude-sonnet-4-6
gemini-pro-latest currently resolves to gemini-3.1-pro-preview

This is a good default rule:

use *-latest when you want the newest capabilities without editing code
use *-stable when you want a more conservative default for shared workflows
use a dated or explicit concrete model ID when you need exact reproducibility



6.1.3 Builder-first workflow
The tidy DSL is useful when you want a reusable chat template rather than a one-off prompt.

quality_chat <- higpt::make_model("gpt-5-mini") |>
  higpt::add_system_prompt("You are a concise data quality assistant.") |>
  higpt::add_user_message("List three first checks for a new orders table.") |>
  higpt::set_reasoning_effort("minimal") |>
  higpt::set_options(temperature = 0.2)

higpt::preview(quality_chat)

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  role  content  
  <chr> <list>   
1 user  <chr [1]>



mock_builder_reply <- function(...) {
  paste(
    "- confirm the primary key is unique",
    "- check order dates are populated and sensible",
    "- compare order totals against expected ranges",
    sep = "\n"
  )
}

builder_result <- with_mocked_namespace_function("higpt", "continue_chat", mock_builder_reply, {
  quality_chat |>
    higpt::submit()
})

higpt::collect_text(builder_result)

[1] "- confirm the primary key is unique\n- check order dates are populated and sensible\n- compare order totals against expected ranges"




6.1.4 Structured outputs with schema builders
The make_schema() / add_field() / finalize_schema() pattern is one of the most useful additions in the newer higpt material because it makes it easier to connect LLM outputs to downstream tables.

check_schema <- higpt::make_schema("quality_check") |>
  higpt::add_field("check_name", "string") |>
  higpt::add_field("why_it_matters", "string") |>
  higpt::finalize_schema()

schema_result <- with_mocked_namespace_function("higpt", "continue_chat", function(...) {
  paste(
    "```json",
    "{\"check_name\":\"primary key uniqueness\",\"why_it_matters\":\"Duplicate order IDs break joins, aggregations, and downstream QA.\"}",
    "```",
    sep = "\n"
  )
}, {
  higpt::make_model("gpt-5-mini") |>
    higpt::add_user_message("Name one important validation for an orders table.") |>
    higpt::set_schema(check_schema, multiple_rows = FALSE) |>
    higpt::submit()
})

schema_result |>
  higpt::enforce_schema() |>
  higpt::collect_df()

              check_name
1 primary key uniqueness
                                                     why_it_matters
1 Duplicate order IDs break joins, aggregations, and downstream QA.




6.1.5 Tools with the builder

add_numbers <- higpt::make_tool("add_numbers", "Add two numbers together") |>
  higpt::add_parameter("a", "number", required = TRUE) |>
  higpt::add_parameter("b", "number", required = TRUE) |>
  higpt::finalize_tool()

handlers <- list(
  add_numbers = function(args) as.character(args$a + args$b)
)

mock_tool_builder <- function(...) {
  list(
    content = "The answer is 42.",
    response = list(
      usage = list(input_tokens = 10, output_tokens = 5)
    )
  )
}

tool_result <- with_mocked_namespace_function("higpt", "chat_with_tools", mock_tool_builder, {
  higpt::make_model("gpt-5-mini") |>
    higpt::add_user_message("What is 12 + 30? Use the tool.") |>
    higpt::add_tool(add_numbers) |>
    higpt::set_tool_choice("auto") |>
    higpt::set_tool_handlers(handlers) |>
    higpt::submit()
})

higpt::collect_text(tool_result)

[1] "The answer is 42."




6.1.6 Prompt files and row-wise workflows
Once prompts become team assets, keep them on disk rather than burying them in scripts. higpt now supports prompt templates, prompt-schema bundles, and row-wise workflows like mutate_llm().

prompt_file <- tempfile(fileext = ".md")
writeLines(
  c(
    "You are supporting the {{team}} team.",
    "Summarise the main risks for {{topic}} in two bullets."
  ),
  prompt_file
)

templated_chat <- higpt::make_model("gpt-5-mini") |>
  higpt::set_prompt_template(prompt_file, role = "user") |>
  higpt::apply_prompt_template(list(team = "operations", topic = "late deliveries"))

higpt::preview(templated_chat)

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  role  content  
  <chr> <list>   
1 user  <chr [1]>



risk_schema <- higpt::make_schema("risk_summary") |>
  higpt::add_field("topic", "string") |>
  higpt::add_array_field("actions", "string") |>
  higpt::finalize_schema()

bundle <- higpt::bundle_prompt_schema(prompt_file, risk_schema)

bundle$prompt_path

[1] "/var/folders/br/z454x1nx287fg3mq63vg3r600000gn/T//RtmpPZL2gV/filea08e55cfb4a4.md"

names(bundle$schema$fields)

[1] "topic"   "actions"



driver_notes <- data.frame(
  metric = c("stockouts", "late deliveries", "returns"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

mock_rowwise_reply <- function(chat_messages, ...) {
  paste("Analyst note:", chat_messages$content[[1]])
}

notes <- with_mocked_namespace_function("higpt", "continue_chat", mock_rowwise_reply, {
  higpt::mutate_llm(
    paste("Write one business interpretation for", driver_notes$metric),
    model_name = "gpt-5-mini",
    .progress = FALSE
  )
})

data.frame(metric = driver_notes$metric, note = notes)

           metric
1       stockouts
2 late deliveries
3         returns
                                                                 note
1       Analyst note: Write one business interpretation for stockouts
2 Analyst note: Write one business interpretation for late deliveries
3         Analyst note: Write one business interpretation for returns


If your team uses local or OpenAI-compatible endpoints, register_custom_model() and list_endpoint_models() are worth learning as well because the same builder patterns then work against internal infrastructure too.


6.1.7 Try it
This exercise needs an API key.

Use get_available_models() to find one model with structured outputs and one with vision support.
Build a make_model() template for a data-quality review.
Add a schema and extract a one-row tibble with enforce_schema() |> collect_df().



6.1.8 Stretch
Create a small reusable workflow that includes all of these:

a prompt file on disk
a schema built with make_schema()
either mutate_llm() across several rows or a custom tool built with make_tool()
optional logging with enable_logging()

title: 5  AI-Era Extensions
section: 6.2 higrate: from data frames to reusable knowledge
text:
6.2 higrate: from data frames to reusable knowledge
higrate is one of the most interesting newer packages because it connects classic data profiling with AI-generated narratives and relationship discovery.

6.2.1 The core move
Take in-memory tables and turn them into:

candidate keys
summary statistics
relationship suggestions
reusable knowledge-base entries



6.2.2 Example with no LLM requirement

library(higrate)

orders_dirty <- data.frame(
  record_id = 1:6,
  customer_id = c("C001", "C001", "C003", "C004", "C002", "C002"),
  order_id = c("O100", "O101", "O300", "O400", "O200", "O200"),
  order_date = as.Date(c("2025-01-03", "2025-01-08", "2025-02-01", "2025-02-05", "2025-01-10", "2025-01-10")),
  order_status = c("shipped", "processing", "shipped", "processing", "processing", "cancelled"),
  amount = c(1200, 950, 2000, 500, 850, 850)
)

orders_desc <- higrate::generate_table_description(
  orders_dirty,
  table_name = "orders",
  use_llm = FALSE,
  use_llm_columns = FALSE
)

customers <- data.frame(
  customer_id = c("C001", "C002", "C003"),
  segment = c("retail", "retail", "wholesale"),
  region = c("Gauteng", "Western Cape", "KZN")
)

customers_desc <- higrate::generate_table_description(
  customers,
  table_name = "customers",
  use_llm = FALSE,
  use_llm_columns = FALSE
)

rel <- higrate::generate_relationships(
  orders_desc,
  customers_desc,
  use_llm = FALSE
)

rel$proposed_relationships

  column_name_1 column_name_2 fwd_score bwd_score overlap
1   customer_id   customer_id 0.8333333         1       1




6.2.3 Relationship thinking





flowchart LR
  A["orders"] -->|"customer_id"| B["customers"]
  A -->|"product_id"| C["products"]
  B --> D["customer narrative"]
  C --> E["product narrative"]
  A --> F["table profile"]
  F --> G["knowledge base entry"]








6.2.4 Try it

Build two toy tables with a clear join path.
Run generate_table_description(..., use_llm = FALSE).
Run generate_relationships(..., use_llm = FALSE).
Decide whether the discovered relationship is actually useful.



6.2.5 Stretch
Choose a real project table pair and design a small knowledge base:

which tables deserve narratives?
which relationships matter?
what would you want a new teammate to learn from the saved KB?

title: 5  AI-Era Extensions
section: 6.3 hidrogen: agentic workflows on top of the stack
text:
6.3 hidrogen: agentic workflows on top of the stack
hidrogen is the package to look at when you want orchestrated AI workflows rather than one-off prompts.

6.3.1 What to focus on

run_data_explorer() for exploration
run_model_explorer() for model-oriented workflows
workflow YAML files
prompt files
tool registry design



6.3.2 Example
hidrogen includes its own mock hook, so this chapter can run the exploration workflow without a live model endpoint.

library(hidrogen)

mock_hidrogen_chat <- function(chat_messages, tools, tool_handlers, system_message = NULL, ...) {
  handler_names <- names(tool_handlers)

  if ("casefile_set_domain" %in% handler_names) {
    tool_handlers$casefile_set_domain(list(
      domain = "Vehicle efficiency analysis",
      actors = c("analytics", "fleet managers"),
      kpis = c("mpg")
    ))
    tool_handlers$casefile_add_hypothesis(list(
      title = "Heavier cars consume more fuel",
      statement = "mpg decreases as weight rises",
      rationale = "The deterministic profile already flags wt vs mpg as a strong negative relationship.",
      priority = "high"
    ))
  }

  if ("casefile_record_evidence" %in% handler_names) {
    tool_handlers$casefile_record_evidence(list(
      hypothesis_id = "hypothesis_001",
      summary = "The profile highlights wt vs mpg as one of the strongest negative correlations.",
      quality = "stat_summary"
    ))
  }

  if ("casefile_update_hypothesis" %in% handler_names) {
    tool_handlers$casefile_update_hypothesis(list(
      hypothesis_id = "hypothesis_001",
      status = "supported",
      note = "Deterministic diagnostics already support the direction of effect."
    ))
  }

  if ("submit_exploration_report" %in% handler_names) {
    tool_handlers$submit_exploration_report(list(
      executive_summary = "Exploration complete for a mock fleet-efficiency dataset.",
      key_insights = c("[hypothesis_001] Higher vehicle weight is associated with lower mpg."),
      supporting_evidence = "The deterministic profile flagged wt vs mpg as one of the strongest negative correlations.",
      next_steps = c(
        "Fit a baseline regression on mpg ~ wt.",
        "Check whether transmission still matters after controlling for weight."
      )
    ))
  }

  list(messages = data.frame(role = "assistant", content = "ok", stringsAsFactors = FALSE))
}

hidrogen::hidrogen_set_llm_mock(
  chat_with_tools = mock_hidrogen_chat,
  continue_chat = function(...) ""
)
on.exit(hidrogen::hidrogen_reset_llm_mock(), add = TRUE)

invisible(utils::capture.output({
  exploration <- hidrogen::run_data_explorer(mtcars, sample_rows = 3)
}))

exploration$report$executive_summary

[1] "Goal: identify the hidden drivers of fuel efficiency (mpg) in a general vehicle dataset and surface risks that affect interpretation. Success metrics: identify supported causal and associative hypotheses, quantify their likely impact on mpg, and produce clear next steps to resolve outstanding uncertainties. Why triggered: exploratory analyses (one basic summary and six segment comparison experiments) highlighted weight and related engine-size metrics as dominant signals affecting mpg; several hypotheses were left unresolved because reconciliation/coefficient aggregation and diagnostics were not completed."

exploration$report$key_insights

[1] "Weight is the dominant predictor of fuel efficiency (H001 supported). Evidence: H001, EXP003 (basic_summary) and repeated segment comparisons EXP001–EXP006. Direction and consistency: heavier vehicles → lower mpg across every segment tested. Note: exact coefficient estimates and percent variance explained are not yet reconciled (no final model coefficients available)."                         
[2] "Engine displacement and cylinder count reduce mpg primarily via increased weight (H002 supported). Evidence: H002, segment_compare experiments EXP001, EXP004, EXP006. When vehicle weight is included in models/segment comparisons, the direct negative effect of displacement/cylinder count on mpg is substantially attenuated (observed consistently across segments), indicating mediation by weight."
[3] "Manual transmission does not increase mpg once weight is controlled for (H003 refuted). Evidence: H003, EXP002, EXP005. Any apparent mpg advantage for manual cars in unadjusted comparisons disappears after adjusting for weight and segment."                                                                                                                                                            
[4] "Horsepower’s effect on mpg remains inconclusive and likely confounded by weight and displacement (H004 testing). Evidence: H004, EXP003. The direction and magnitude of horsepower’s association with mpg vary across segments when controlling for other predictors; further multivariate analysis is required to separate direct vs. mediated effects."                                                   
[5] "Small sample size and generalisability concerns remain (H005 proposed). Evidence: H005, EXP003. The dataset and segment-level splits are limited enough that effect estimates may be unstable and sensitive to sampling; this reduces confidence in applying findings broadly."                                                                                                                             




6.3.3 Why it matters
hidrogen teaches a more important lesson than any one workflow: if you want AI systems to be dependable, they need structure, tools, and auditable steps.


6.3.4 Try it

Read one prompt file from hidrogen.
Identify which parts are domain knowledge and which parts are workflow control.
Suggest one improvement to make the workflow safer or clearer.



6.3.5 Stretch
Design a new workflow idea for your team. Example:

data dictionary builder
process incident explainer
first-pass forecast QA agent

Write:

the goal
the required tools
the main failure modes

title: 5  AI-Era Extensions
text:
6.4 Think
Which of these creates more value in practice:

an LLM that can talk about your data
or a workflow that turns data knowledge into reusable team memory?

title: 6  Modeling and Forecasting
text:
7 Where himodel fits
himodel is not the most universal package in the hiverse, but it is important for forecasting and structured modeling work.
It is best understood as a modeling utility package with a strong forecasting flavor, not as a general replacement for all of R’s modeling ecosystem.

title: 6  Modeling and Forecasting
text:
7.1 What it is useful for

forecast error calculation
forecast reshaping and reallocation
helper utilities around time-based model workflows

title: 6  Modeling and Forecasting
text:
7.2 A minimal example

library(himodel)

forecast_eval <- data.frame(
  sku = c("A", "A", "A", "B", "B", "B"),
  actual = c(100, 120, 110, 80, 75, 90),
  forecast = c(95, 130, 100, 82, 70, 88)
)

himodel::compute_error(
  forecast_eval,
  measure_level = c("sku"),
  report_level = c("sku"),
  actual_var = "actual",
  pred_var = "forecast"
)

# A tibble: 2 × 6
  sku       E     A     P     rE      rB
  <chr> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl>   <dbl>
1 A         5   330   325 0.0152 -0.0152
2 B         5   245   240 0.0204 -0.0204

title: 6  Modeling and Forecasting
text:
7.3 Why this matters
A lot of poor forecasting practice starts before the model itself:

wrong aggregation level
poor error reporting
hidden bias
weak hierarchy handling

himodel is useful when you want a standard way to talk about those things.

title: 6  Modeling and Forecasting
text:
7.4 A simple visual

actual <- c(100, 120, 110, 80, 75, 90)
forecast <- c(95, 130, 100, 82, 70, 88)
period <- seq_along(actual)

matplot(
  period,
  cbind(actual, forecast),
  type = "b",
  pch = c(16, 17),
  lty = 1,
  col = c("#0b6e69", "#a44a1f"),
  xlab = "Period",
  ylab = "Value",
  main = "Actuals vs forecast"
)
legend(
  "topright",
  legend = c("Actual", "Forecast"),
  col = c("#0b6e69", "#a44a1f"),
  lty = 1,
  pch = c(16, 17),
  bty = "n"
)

title: 6  Modeling and Forecasting
text:
7.5 Try it

Build a toy forecast-versus-actual dataset for two products or customers.
Compute the error summary.
Identify which series has the worst relative bias.

title: 6  Modeling and Forecasting
text:
7.6 Stretch
Use a monthly series such as AirPassengers or a small project series and ask:

what is the reporting level?
what is the measurement level?
what would a stakeholder want to see in an error report?

Then sketch the table you would want himodel to produce.

title: 6  Modeling and Forecasting
section: 7.7 Where it connects to other packages
text:
7.7 Where it connects to other packages

hidate for time boundaries
himunge for shaping the input data
hipoint for regime shifts and anomalies
higrate if you want AI-generated narratives around modeled tables

title: 6  Modeling and Forecasting
text:
7.8 Think
What is the fastest way to lose stakeholder trust in a forecasting project:

a small model miss
or unclear, inconsistent error reporting?

title: 7  Specialist Packages
text:
8 Two optional packages worth knowing early
Even though they are optional, hipoint and hisched are interesting enough that new employees should at least know what they are for.

title: 7  Specialist Packages
text:
8.1 hipoint: change points and anomalies
hipoint is useful when the core question is not “what is the average trend?” but rather:

when did the regime change?
which observations are suspicious?
should this time series be treated as stable at all?


8.1.1 The package view
The package exposes:

outlier.detect_anomalies()
chpts.detect_changepoints()

plus several specific methods under the hood.


8.1.2 Example

library(hipoint)

set.seed(42)

signal_df <- data.frame(
  date = seq.Date(as.Date("2026-01-01"), by = "day", length.out = 40),
  signal = c(
    rnorm(15, mean = 10, sd = 0.8),
    rnorm(15, mean = 18, sd = 0.8),
    32,
    rnorm(9, mean = 17, sd = 0.8)
  )
)

invisible(utils::capture.output({
  suppressMessages({
    change_points <- hipoint::chpts.detect_changepoints(
      signal_df,
      index = "date",
      signal = "signal",
      method = "intercept"
    )
  })
}))

anomalies <- hipoint::outlier.detect_anomalies(
  signal_df,
  index = "date",
  signal = "signal",
  method = "mad"
)

change_points

[1] "2026-01-15" "2026-01-29" "2026-02-03" "2026-02-09"

anomalies

[1] "2026-01-31"




8.1.3 A visual intuition

signal_df$idx <- seq_len(nrow(signal_df))

plot(
  signal_df$idx,
  signal_df$signal,
  type = "b",
  pch = 16,
  col = "#0b6e69",
  xlab = "Index",
  ylab = "Signal",
  main = "A synthetic series with a regime shift and one obvious anomaly"
)
abline(v = 15.5, lty = 2, col = "#a44a1f")
points(31, signal_df$signal[31], pch = 19, col = "#a44a1f", cex = 1.3)
legend(
  "topleft",
  legend = c("Signal", "Candidate changepoint", "Candidate anomaly"),
  col = c("#0b6e69", "#a44a1f", "#a44a1f"),
  lty = c(1, 2, NA),
  pch = c(16, NA, 19),
  bty = "n"
)










8.1.4 Try it

Create a series with one level shift and one outlier.
Run both chpts.detect_changepoints() and outlier.detect_anomalies().
Compare whether the methods agree with your visual intuition.



8.1.5 Stretch
Take a real business metric that should be fairly stable. Ask:

what counts as drift?
what counts as a one-off anomaly?
what should trigger human investigation?

title: 7  Specialist Packages
section: 8.2 hisched: heuristic scheduling and timetable improvement
text:
8.2 hisched: heuristic scheduling and timetable improvement
hisched is optional because it is domain-specific, but it is one of the more substantial specialist packages in the ecosystem. It is recent, large, and well tested.

8.2.1 What it is for
hisched helps build and improve schedules, especially in school or timetable contexts where:

teacher constraints matter
student clashes matter
feasible is not the same as optimal
fast improvement heuristics are more practical than exact global optimisation



8.2.2 The package workflow





flowchart LR
  A["Enrollments"] --> B["make_schedule_problem()"]
  C["Scheduler inputs"] --> B
  B --> D["create_initial_schedule()"]
  D --> E["score() / validate()"]
  E --> F["do_annealing()"]
  F --> G["repair_sequence()"]
  G --> H["export or inspect"]








8.2.3 Example with the package sample data

suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(hisched))

set.seed(123)

sched_input <- hisched::prepare_sched_input(
  sched_input = hisched::sched_input_sample,
  target_date = as.Date("2026-01-12"),
  school = "IEB",
  phase = "FET"
)

invisible(utils::capture.output({
  problem <- hisched::make_schedule_problem(
    enrol = hisched::enrol_sample,
    sched_input = sched_input,
    target_date = as.Date("2026-01-12"),
    school = "IEB",
    phase = "FET"
  )

  problem <- hisched::create_initial_schedule(problem, verbose = FALSE)
  problem <- hisched::score(problem, engine = "r")
  problem <- hisched::validate(problem)
}))

problem$score$total_cost

[1] 10021639

head(problem$issues[, c("type", "severity", "explanation")])

                       type severity
1 assigned_class_incomplete       OK
2                atom_split       OK
3    best_teacher_not_first    score
4    best_teacher_not_first    score
5    best_teacher_not_first    score
6    best_teacher_not_first    score
                                                                                                                                                     explanation
1                                                                                                     All assigned classes are scheduled with lesson targets met
2                                                                                                                                        No atom splits detected
3 Teacher KUYS (best_teacher) for course 'IEB-E-math-12' teaches lesson 1 at Tue 13 Jan 12:45, but BJEO (another teacher) teaches it earlier at Mon 12 Jan 09:45
4 Teacher KUYS (best_teacher) for course 'IEB-E-math-12' teaches lesson 3 at Wed 14 Jan 11:15, but BJEO (another teacher) teaches it earlier at Mon 12 Jan 15:25
5 Teacher KUYS (best_teacher) for course 'IEB-E-math-12' teaches lesson 5 at Thu 15 Jan 16:10, but BJEO (another teacher) teaches it earlier at Wed 14 Jan 10:30
6 Teacher BJEO (best_teacher) for course 'IEB-E-phsc-10' teaches lesson 2 at Tue 13 Jan 15:25, but PMUR (another teacher) teaches it earlier at Mon 12 Jan 12:45




8.2.4 Why this package is interesting even if you never schedule timetables
It is a good example of:

heuristic search
simulated annealing
scoring design
balancing feasibility against improvement

That makes it worth reading as an engineering package, not only a domain package.


8.2.5 Try it

Read the hisched README and map the main stages of the algorithm.
Load the sample data.
Identify which objects are inputs, which are state, and which are outputs.



8.2.6 Stretch
Suppose a stakeholder says:
“We do not need the globally best schedule. We need a clearly better schedule by tomorrow, and we need to understand why it improved.”
Write a short note explaining why a heuristic package like hisched may be a better fit than a purely exact optimizer.

title: 7  Specialist Packages
text:
8.3 Think
What do hipoint and hisched have in common?
They both help in settings where the real problem is not just prediction. It is decision support under imperfect structure.

title: 8  Supporting Packages at a Glance
text:
9 The rest of the toolbox
You do not need a full chapter for every package, but you should know the supporting cast.
crumbs: 8  Supporting Packages at a Glance

title: 8  Supporting Packages at a Glance
text:
9.1 Quick catalog









Package
Main use
Who should care first?
Current role




hiplot
Hudson plotting theme and palettes
Analysts making internal plots
Lightweight but handy


hilog
Simple logging
Everyone writing reusable analysis, scripts, or processes
Important early-use helper; see the core chapter


hifile
File import/export helpers
Ingestion and reporting work
Useful support package


hidoc
R Markdown and data-dictionary helpers
Reporting-heavy work
Useful, but rougher docs


hidash
Dashboard utilities
Shiny dashboard builders
Niche but useful


himatch
String matching and standardisation
Data quality / matching work
Good utility package


higest
Structured ingestion pipelines
Process authors
Platform-oriented


hidalgo
General algorithms/data structures
Package developers, niche use
Older and smaller
crumbs: 8  Supporting Packages at a Glance

title: 8  Supporting Packages at a Glance
text:
9.2 A few package heuristics

9.2.1 Reach for hiplot when:

the plot is internally useful but visually inconsistent
you want an Isazi/Hudson house style quickly



9.2.2 Reach for hilog when:

you want your script to explain what it is doing while it runs
you are writing a package or process that needs explicit logs
you want debug/info/error behavior without inventing your own logger



9.2.3 Reach for himatch when:

entity names are messy
you need standardisation before joining or grouping
stakeholders keep saying “these should match, but they do not”



9.2.4 Reach for hidoc when:

you need data dictionaries
you are building R Markdown outputs for others
crumbs: 8  Supporting Packages at a Glance

title: 8  Supporting Packages at a Glance
text:
9.3 Try it
Pick one of these supporting packages and answer:

what problem does it remove?
what would you do without it?
is it general-purpose or workflow-specific?
crumbs: 8  Supporting Packages at a Glance

title: 8  Supporting Packages at a Glance
text:
9.4 Think
A package can be useful without being central. Which supporting package do you think you are most likely to underuse at first?
crumbs: 8  Supporting Packages at a Glance

title: 9  Capstone Exercises
text:
10 Pull the pieces together
These capstone exercises are meant to feel more like real Isazi work and less like isolated package demos.

title: 9  Capstone Exercises
text:
10.1 Capstone 1: first-contact data audit
Goal: inspect an unfamiliar table and decide whether it is trustworthy enough for downstream use.
Suggested stack:

hiconnect
himunge
hilog
hidate if dates are involved


10.1.1 Task

Read a table from Hudson, or simulate one locally.
Propose one or more candidate keys with himunge::find_primary_keys().
Use himunge::find_indices_violating_key() to identify suspicious rows.
Write a one-page audit note with:

candidate key
key violations
missing-value concerns
date issues
whether the table is fit for modeling

Add hilog::info() messages so the audit leaves a readable execution trace.

title: 9  Capstone Exercises
section: 10.2 Capstone 2: build a mini knowledge base
text:
10.2 Capstone 2: build a mini knowledge base
Goal: move from “I explored these two tables once” to “the team can reuse what I learned”.
Suggested stack:

himunge
higrate
optionally higpt


10.2.1 Task

Create or load two related tables.
Generate descriptions for each.
Discover the relationship between them.
Save the result mentally or on disk as if it were the start of a real KB.
Write a short note explaining what a new joiner should know about these tables.

title: 9  Capstone Exercises
text:
10.3 Capstone 3: platform incident walkthrough
Goal: reason about a surprising downstream output.
Suggested stack:

hiproc
hiconnect
himunge


10.3.1 Task
Imagine a stakeholder says:
“Yesterday’s dashboard looked fine. Today’s looks wrong.”
Work through:

which process likely refreshed the output?
what upstream dependencies should you inspect?
which table would you validate first?
what quick himunge checks would you run after pulling the data?

title: 9  Capstone Exercises
text:
10.4 Capstone 4: AI-assisted exploration with guardrails
Goal: use the AI packages without surrendering judgment.
Suggested stack:

higpt
higrate
hidrogen


10.4.1 Task

Ask higpt to produce a structured exploration plan for a small dataset.
Use higrate to profile the table heuristically.
Compare the human view, the heuristic view, and the LLM view.
Decide what an agentic workflow should automate and what should stay manual.

title: 9  Capstone Exercises
text:
10.5 Capstone 5: specialist deep dive
Choose one:

hipoint for change points and anomalies
hisched for heuristic optimisation and timetable design


10.5.1 Task for hipoint

Create a signal with a regime change and a few suspicious spikes.
Detect change points.
Detect anomalies.
Explain where algorithmic output and domain intuition disagree.



10.5.2 Task for hisched

Study the sample scheduling inputs.
Build the initial problem object.
Explain what the objective is trying to balance.
Write a short brief for a non-technical stakeholder describing why a heuristic approach is reasonable.

title: 9  Capstone Exercises
text:
10.6 Final reflection
By this point you should be able to answer:

Which package would I reach for first?
Which packages are infrastructure rather than analysis tools?
Which tasks are ready for AI assistance, and which still need strong human judgment?

If you cannot answer those yet, go back to the earlier chapters and rerun the small exercises with your own data.
